pattern: Since every row loses exactly one element per round and only the largest remaining value of a row can ever be the one removed, sorting each row up front turns the whole simulation into just reading off, for every round from the back, the biggest value sitting at that position across all rows.
hints: Simulating the removal round by round with a live max-search in each row works, but you can avoid repeated scanning by sorting every row once at the start.
After sorting a row ascending, its largest remaining value in round k (0-indexed) is always at a fixed position counted from the end of that row.
So the answer is the sum, over each position counted from the end, of the maximum value sitting at that position across all rows — no simulation of removal needed at all.

constraints.note: every row has the same length, between 1 and 500

edge_inputs.description: a 4x3 matrix worked through several rounds of removals
a single row with a single element
a single row with several elements, so every round only ever has one candidate
several single-element rows, so there is only one round total
a matrix of all zeros, exercising the minimum allowed value
tied maximum values across rows in the same round, where it should not matter which is picked as the removed value
a matrix using the maximum allowed cell value
a row that is already sorted descending versus one that is already sorted ascending, to catch a sort-direction mistake

stress.description: the largest allowed matrix, 300 rows by 500 columns, with random cell values across the full allowed range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m, n = 300, 500
    nums = [[rng.randint(0, 1000) for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(m)]
    return [nums]
